content.full_text:
Re:
Fern PoLrrrcAL PnecrrcEs CouMrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
March 4,2003
Nicole A. Tutt
Nossaman, Guthner, Knox
& Elliott, LLP
50 California Street -34do Floor
San Francisco, CA 94111-4707
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-02-341
Dear Ms. Tutt:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Dr. John Gerhardt
Eiskamp, Director of Division D, Pajaro valley water Management Agency
("PVV[MA") regardingthe conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the'Act").' This letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have
abeady taken place. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission') does not
act as a finder of fact when it renders advice; this advice is applicable and confers
immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the
material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FppC Ops. 7l; Govt. Code
section 83114.)
QUESTTONS
l. Does Dr. Eiskamp have a conflict of interest disqualifring him from making,
participating in making, or influencing the decisions of the PWIMA concerning
PWIMA's collection action against William J. McGrath?
2. Does Dr. Eiskamp have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from making,
participating in making, or influencing the decisions of the PW\rMA concerning its
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
:::


File No. A-02-341
Page No. 2
defense in a suit brought against it by James Scurich and others regarding the legality of
the PVWMA' s "augmentation char ge"?
CONCLUSIONS
l. & 2. Yes. Dr. Eiskamp has a conflict of interest disqualifying him from
involvement in the decisions ofthe PWITMA concerning these suits. Such decisions will
have areasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Mr. McGrath who, through
W.J. McGrath Orchards, is a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp.
FACTS
PVWMA is apublic entity and a California Water Agency orgarized and
operating under Chapter 124 of the Califomia Water Code. PVWMA was formed,
among other reasons, to manage the ground and surface water resources within the pajaro
Basin, situated within Monterey, santa cruz and, San Benito counties.
According to the facts you supplied in a February 6,2003, telephone conversation
with the Commission's stafi PV![MA's jurisdiction extends over approximately 70,000
acres, of which 30,000 acres are irrigated for agricultural use. You further stated that
approximately 58,000 acre-feet of groundwater is used annually within PW1rMA's
jurisdiction, which is extracted through approximately 800 privately-owned wells. This
water supply is augmented by an additional2,000 acre-feet per year of surface water. Of
the total, approximately 10,000 acre-feet of groundwater per year is used to supply
municipal water, with the remainder supplying the agricultural needs of the jurisdiction.
Dr. Eiskamp owns a parcel of real estate located in Santa Cruz County.
Approximately 17 acres of this property is leased to W.J. McGrath Orchards pursuant to a
6-year lease signed by Mr. McGrath, which expires in2004. Payment under ihe lease is
$17,000 annually. These 17 acres are subleased to Justin Brown for agricultural uses.
Mr. McGrath supplies irrigation water to the 17 acres, obtained from a groundwater
extraction facility located on his adjoining parcel ofreal estate.
By a complaint dated April 30, 2002, PVWMA corlmenced a collection action
against Mr. McGrath for $36,524.49 in allegedly unpaid groundwater augmentation
charges, dating back to January 1997 ("collection suit"). These charges relate to Mr.
McGrath's pumping and production of water from the groundwater extraction facilities
he owns on his property, located adjacent to Dr. Eiskamp's santa cruz property.
Dr. Eiskamp, in conjunction with his son (John Eiskamp) and four other family
members, jointly owns a 120-acre parcel of real estate located in Monterey County,
within the jurisdiction of PWIMA. A 50-acre portion of this property is leased to Faurot
Farms, a separate entity unrelated to any of these six owners, for an annual rent of
$8,000. Faurot Farms uses this leasehold property for agricultural purposes. Irrigation
water is supplied to Faurot Farms from groundwater extracted through facilities.lointty


File No. A-02-341
Page No. 3
owned by the six Eiskamp family members/property owners.' The remainder of this
parcel contains two residences, leased for $75 and $800 per month, respectively, as well
as pasture land used for granngthe Eiskamp family's horses.
Dr. Eiskamp's adult son, John Eiskamp, together with Mr. McGrath and three
others, are plaintiffs in a suit filed against PWIMA (James P. Scurich, John E. Eislmmp,
Ytncent J. Gizdich III, Dick Peixoto and Wtlliam J. McGrath v. PVWMA, Santa Cruz
County Superior Court, CY 144843, filed October 24,2002.) The suit challenges the
constitutionality of the groundwater augmentation charges imposed jurisdiction-wide by
PVWMA.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (bxl) - (8), which is discussed below. The general ru|e,
however, is that a conflict of interest may occur whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision which may have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on one or more of his or her economic interests.
l. &2. rs Dr. Eiskamp a public official making participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. A "public
official" means:
"everyt mernber, officer, anployee or consultant of a state or local government
agency, but does not include judges and court commissioners in the judicial
branch of govemment. . ." (Section 82048.)
For this pu{pose, a "member" is defined as follows:
"(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried
members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking
authority. A committee, board or commission possesses decisionmaking
authority whenever:
"(A) It may make a final governmental decision;
2 In your February 6,2003,telephone conversation with the Commission's staff, you clarified that
pursuant to relevant code sections, joint owners of groundwater exkaction facilities located in PV![MA,s
jurisdiction are jointly and severally liable for water charges, including groundwater augmentation charges.


File No. A-02-341
Page No. 4
"(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or itmay prevent a
governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive power to initiate
the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be overridden; or
"(c) It makes substantive recommendations that arc, and over an extended
period of time have been, regularly approved without significant
amendment or modification by anotherpublic official or governmental
agency ;' (Regulation I 8701 (aXl ).)
As a district director for PVWMA, Dr. Eiskamp is a public official (see above).
Unless disqualified under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, Dr.
Eiskamp will make, participate in making, and influence governmental decisions,
including decisions regarding litigation in the two above-described matters. (Section
87100; regulations 18702.1 - 18702.3.)
3. What are Dr. Eiskamp's economic interests?
The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined in
regulatiols 18703-18703.5. Based on the facts you supply, the specific economic
interests3 that may apply to Dr. Eiskamp are:
Real Property -- Apublic official has an economic interest in any real property in
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair
market value. (Section 87103(b); reg. 18703.2.) Forpurposes of section 87103,"indirect
investment or interest" means "any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a
business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent
children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a lO-percent interest or greater."
Santa Cruz Parcel: Dr. Eiskamp owns a large agricultural real estate parcel in
Santa CnlzCowfiy. Therefore, presumably his interest in this property has a value of
$2,000 or more, making this property an economic interest to him within the meaning of
the Act.
Monterey Parcel: Dr. Eiskamp is also a one-sixth owner of a 120-acre real
estate parcel, consisting chiefly of agricultural and pasture lands, located in Monterey
County. We assume that his pro-ratainterest in this property has a value of $2,000 or
more, making this property an economic interest to him within the meaning of the Act.
Business Entity -- A public official has an economic interest in any business entity
in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more, or if the
3 In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87ll3,a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period. (Regulation
18703.s.)


File No. A-02-341
Page No. 5
official is a director, officer, parhrer, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management in the business entity. (Regulations 1 8703. 1 (a) and (b).) For purposes of
regulation 1 8703.1, the term "business entity" refers to "any organrzation or enterprise
operated for profit, including...a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint
venture, syndicate, corporation or association." (Section 82005.)
We have previously advised that when two or more individuals own a piece of
real property for the pu{pose of making a profit, this is a joint venture4 recognLed as a
"business entity" under the Act. (Daniel Advice Letter,No. 4-00-134; Witson Advice
Lette4 No. 4-98-148.) Dr. Eiskamp, together with 5 other members of the Eiskamp
farnlly,jointly own the Monterey County real estate, portions of which are leased to
others and provide income. For the reasons stated above, this joint ownership is a joint
venture and business entity within the meaning of the Act. Assuming that Dr. Eiskamp,s
direct or indirect investment in this business entity has a value of $2,000 or more, or tliat
he exercises a position of management with respect to the rental or other business actiity
on this parcel, this business entity is an economic interest to him.
Sources of Income -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision at issue. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3). Income, for this
pu{pose, includes apro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which
the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a l0 percent or
greater interest. (Section 8203 0(a).)
The Eiskamp Family Business (Monterey county parcel):s The Eiskamp
family business entity (above) managing the Monterey County parcel is a source of
income to Dr. Eiskamp, by virtue of its distribution of rental proceeds to him.
Sources of Income to the Eiskamp Family Business: Since Dr. Eiskamp is a
greater than 10 percant owner in the Eiskamp family business, sources of income to the
business (e.g., Faurot Farms and the residential tenant on the property paying rent of
4^
-'lhe common definition of a'Joint venture" is a'ospecial combination of two or more persons,
yhgre, in some specific venture, a profit is jointly sought without any actual partnership or corporate
designation, or as an association ofpersons to carry out a single business entirprise foiprofit, ior which
purpose they combine their property, money, effects, skill and knowledge." (Sadugor i. Holstein (1962)
199 Cal.App.2d 477 , 483.)
5 The Commission has advised previously that in certain circumstances the nominal structure of a
business entity may be "pierced" when ownership or control of the entity is vested in certain persons. (1n
re Lumsdon (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 140.) When this piercing occursi, the economic interest is diemed to exist
in the owning or controlling person(s) as well as the business entity. (In re Nord (1983) 8 FppC Ops. 6;
Hentschke Advice Letter, No. 4-80-069.) However, we have advised thatpiercing does not apply io
partnerships that have more than two parhrers if no single partner holds a controlling position or acts as a
managingpartner. (HahnAdviceLetter,No.I-91-311.) SinceDr.Eiskamp'sjointvinturehas5other
members and the facts do not indicate that one or two of the Eiskamp family members hold a conholling
position or act as a managing parbrer, it does not appear that "piercing" is appropriate.


File No. A-02-341
Page No. 6
$800 per month) are also sources of income to him, to the extent that his pro ratashare of
the business entity's income from these sources is $500 or greater. 6 (Section 82030(a). )
Sources of fncome - Santa Craz Parcel: Mr. McGrath is the owner and manager
of a sole proprietorship, W.J. McGrath Orchards, which is the lessee of a portion of Dr.
Eiskamp's Santa Cruzpropefi. Since Mr. McGrath, as the sole proprietor controls the
relationship between W.J. McGrath Orchards and Dr. Eiskamp, and that relationship
provides rental income to Dr. Eiskamp, Mr. McGrath is a source of income to Dr.
Eiskamp, along with W.J. McGrath Orchards. (Hentschke Advice Letter,No. 4-80-069;
Korb Advice Letter, No. A-99-113.)
4. Will these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
OUESTION 1: PVWMA COLLECTION ACTION AGAINST MR. McGRATH
Business Entity/Sources of Income
A business entity or source of income in which apublic official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if it initiates the proceeding by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similarrequest, or is a named party in, or is the
subject of the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(l)-(a)(2).) A business entity or source of income is the
subject of a proceeding concerning the decision before the agency if the "decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with," the business entity or source of income.
(Subdivision 18704.l(a\Q).) If the business entity or source of income is not directly
involved, then it is deemed to be indirectly involved in the govemmental decision.
(Regulation I 870a. I @).)
Mr. McGrath (a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp) is a named parfy in the collection
suit and as such, is directly involved in any decisions made by PVWMA regarding the
suit.
a
o
Dr. Eiskamp's remaining economic interests are indirectly tnvolved in decisions
conceming the collection suit.
6 As noted above, a public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investrnent of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or of which
he or she is a director, officer, parfirer, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section
87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b)). The material you provide contains a copy of the lease agreement
between Mr. McGrath and Dr. Eiskamp concerning the Santa Cruz property. This agreement discloses the
existence of the Eiskamp Family Limited Parbrership. The facts do not discloseo however, whether this
limited parhership is presently in existence, and if so, if it is a business entity that is an economic interest
to Dr. Eiskamp under the foregoing standards. Dr. Eiskamp should consider this question in determining
his economic interests under the Act.


File No. A-02-341
Page No. 7
Interests in Real Property
The general rule is that an official's interest in real property is considered to be
directly involved in a decision if it meets any of the criteria in regulation 18704.2(a).
Regulation 1870a.2@) provides that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if "[t]he real property in
which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is located within 500
feet of the boundaries (or the_proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of
the governmental decision." T The real property that is thcsubjeci of the collection suit is
Mr. McGrath's property on which is located the groundwaterpumping facilities subject
to the augmentation charges for which collection is sought. This property adjoins Dr.
Eiskamp's santa Cnnpropefi. Since Dr. Eiskamp's Santa cruzproperfi is located
within 500 feet of the boundaries of the property which is the subject of governmental
decision, the Santa Cruz property is deemed tobe directly involved in PV![MA's
decisions concerning the collection suit.
Since Dr. Eiskamp's interest in his jointly-owned real property in Monterey
County does not meet any of the provisions of regulation 18704.2(a) his interest in this
real property is deemed tobe indirectly involved in such decisions. (Regulation
t87oa.2@)Q).)
OUESTION 2: GROUNDWATER AUGMENTATION CHARGE SUIT
Under the criteria above, Mr. McGrath (a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp) is
also directly involved in PWIMA's decisions concerning this suit. All Dr. Eiskamp's
other economic interests are indirectly involved.
Steps 5. & 6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of these governmental decisions upon Dr.
Eiskamp's economic interests wiII meet this materiality standard?
According to your facts, Dr. Eiskamp has two economic interests that are directly
involved in collection-related litigation decisions. The first is Mr. McGrath and the
second is Dr. Eiskamp's Santa Cruzparcel.
Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect at all, even one pemy, on an
individual such as Mr. McGrath, who is a source of income to apublic official, when that
individual is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed
material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).) With respect to real property, regulation
18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on directly
involved real property that is an economic interest to a public official is presumed to be
7 Amendments to regulation 187}4.2were adopted by the Commission at its January 17,2003,
public meeting. The above-quoted laaguage is taken from amended regulation 18704.2(a), as adopted by
the Commission.


File No. A-02-341
Page No. 8
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the governmental decision will have any financial effect at all on the real property.
Any proof relied upon by a public official in this regard must be reasonable and
objective.
Underregulation 18706, an effect upon economic interests is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. A financial
effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 19S.)
Since, under the facts provided, the outcome of this suit will likely determine Mr.
McGrath's liability to pay approximately $36,000 in past due charges, decisions
concerning the prosecution of this collection action will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Mr. McGrath. Consequently, Dr. Eiskamp has a conflict of
interest disqualifoing him from making,participating in making, or influencing the
PVWMA's decisions concerning the collections suit. Having reached this result, it is
unnecessary to discuss in our advice the reasonably foreseeable material financial effects
of such decisions on Dr. Eiskamp's other directly and indirectly involved economic
interests.
OUESTION 2: GROUNDWATER AUGMENTATION CHARGE SUIT
Mr. McGrath is directly involved as a co-plaintiffin this suit. As noted above, he
is a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp and thus, if decisions conceming the groundwater
augmentation charge suit will have any reasonably foreseeable financial effect at all, even
one penny, upon Mr. McGrath, it is deemed material. Since this suit, like the suit above,
bears upon Mr. McGrath's liability to pay approximately $36,000 in groundwater
augmentation charges, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions by PVWMA
concerning its defense of this suit will have amaterial financial effect upon Mr. McGrath.
Having reached this result, it is unnecessary to discuss in our advice the
reasonably foreseeable material financial ef,flects of such decisions on Dr. Eiskamp,s
other directly and indirectly involved economic interests.
7. & 8. "Public Generally" and'6legally Required participation, Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Section 87103, regulation 18707(a).) Under this
exception, if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is affected by the governmental
decision in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public official, then the
official may participate in the decision. Step eight is an exception (regulation 18708) that
applies when the official is legally required to participate in the decision. Typically, this
exception is invoked when a quorum cannot be constituted without the participation of
the disqualified official. There is nothing in the facts you supply to suggest that either of
these exceptions apply to Dr. Eiskamp's involvement in these decisions. We have
enclosed for your information copies of regulations 18707, 18707.1, and,18707.2, which


File No. A-02-341
Page No. 9
address the public generally exception, and regulation 18708, which addresses the legally
required participation exception.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
KLGje

2. Does Dr. Eiskamp have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from making,
participating in making, or influencing the decisions of the PW\rMA concerning its
:::
defense in a suit brought against it by James Scurich and others regarding the legality of
the PVWMA' s "augmentation char ge"?

involvement in the decisions ofthe PWITMA concerning these suits. Such decisions will
have areasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Mr. McGrath who, through
W.J. McGrath Orchards, is a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp.

or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in

authority. A committee, board or commission possesses decisionmaking
authority whenever:

governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive power to initiate
the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be overridden; or

period of time have been, regularly approved without significant
amendment or modification by anotherpublic official or governmental
agency ;' (Regulation I 8701 (aXl ).)
As a district director for PVWMA, Dr. Eiskamp is a public official (see above).

Eiskamp will make, participate in making, and influence governmental decisions,
including decisions regarding litigation in the two above-described matters. (Section
87100; regulations 18702.1 - 18702.3.)

regulatiols 18703-18703.5. Based on the facts you supply, the specific economic

Real Property -- Apublic official has an economic interest in any real property in
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair

in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more, or if the

official is a director, officer, parhrer, trustee, employee, or holds any position of

venture, syndicate, corporation or association." (Section 82005.)
We have previously advised that when two or more individuals own a piece of
real property for the pu{pose of making a profit, this is a joint venture4 recognLed as a
"business entity" under the Act. (Daniel Advice Letter,No. 4-00-134; Witson Advice
Lette4 No. 4-98-148.) Dr. Eiskamp, together with 5 other members of the Eiskamp
farnlly,jointly own the Monterey County real estate, portions of which are leased to
others and provide income. For the reasons stated above, this joint ownership is a joint
venture and business entity within the meaning of the Act. Assuming that Dr. Eiskamp,s
direct or indirect investment in this business entity has a value of $2,000 or more, or tliat
he exercises a position of management with respect to the rental or other business actiity
on this parcel, this business entity is an economic interest to him.
Sources of Income -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision at issue. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3). Income, for this
pu{pose, includes apro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which
the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a l0 percent or
greater interest. (Section 8203 0(a).)
The Eiskamp Family Business (Monterey county parcel):s The Eiskamp
family business entity (above) managing the Monterey County parcel is a source of
income to Dr. Eiskamp, by virtue of its distribution of rental proceeds to him.
Sources of Income to the Eiskamp Family Business: Since Dr. Eiskamp is a
greater than 10 percant owner in the Eiskamp family business, sources of income to the
business (e.g., Faurot Farms and the residential tenant on the property paying rent of
4^
-'lhe common definition of a'Joint venture" is a'ospecial combination of two or more persons,
yhgre, in some specific venture, a profit is jointly sought without any actual partnership or corporate
designation, or as an association ofpersons to carry out a single business entirprise foiprofit, ior which
purpose they combine their property, money, effects, skill and knowledge." (Sadugor i. Holstein (1962)
199 Cal.App.2d 477 , 483.)
5 The Commission has advised previously that in certain circumstances the nominal structure of a
business entity may be "pierced" when ownership or control of the entity is vested in certain persons. (1n
re Lumsdon (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 140.) When this piercing occursi, the economic interest is diemed to exist
in the owning or controlling person(s) as well as the business entity. (In re Nord (1983) 8 FppC Ops. 6;
Hentschke Advice Letter, No. 4-80-069.) However, we have advised thatpiercing does not apply io
partnerships that have more than two parhrers if no single partner holds a controlling position or acts as a
managingpartner. (HahnAdviceLetter,No.I-91-311.) SinceDr.Eiskamp'sjointvinturehas5other
members and the facts do not indicate that one or two of the Eiskamp family members hold a conholling
position or act as a managing parbrer, it does not appear that "piercing" is appropriate.
$800 per month) are also sources of income to him, to the extent that his pro ratashare of
the business entity's income from these sources is $500 or greater. 6 (Section 82030(a). )
Sources of fncome - Santa Craz Parcel: Mr. McGrath is the owner and manager
of a sole proprietorship, W.J. McGrath Orchards, which is the lessee of a portion of Dr.
Eiskamp's Santa Cruzpropefi. Since Mr. McGrath, as the sole proprietor controls the
relationship between W.J. McGrath Orchards and Dr. Eiskamp, and that relationship
provides rental income to Dr. Eiskamp, Mr. McGrath is a source of income to Dr.
Eiskamp, along with W.J. McGrath Orchards. (Hentschke Advice Letter,No. 4-80-069;
Korb Advice Letter, No. A-99-113.)
4. Will these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
OUESTION 1: PVWMA COLLECTION ACTION AGAINST MR. McGRATH
Business Entity/Sources of Income
A business entity or source of income in which apublic official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if it initiates the proceeding by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similarrequest, or is a named party in, or is the
subject of the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(l)-(a)(2).) A business entity or source of income is the
subject of a proceeding concerning the decision before the agency if the "decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with," the business entity or source of income.
(Subdivision 18704.l(a\Q).) If the business entity or source of income is not directly
involved, then it is deemed to be indirectly involved in the govemmental decision.
(Regulation I 870a. I @).)
Mr. McGrath (a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp) is a named parfy in the collection
suit and as such, is directly involved in any decisions made by PVWMA regarding the
suit.
a
o
Dr. Eiskamp's remaining economic interests are indirectly tnvolved in decisions
conceming the collection suit.
6 As noted above, a public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investrnent of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or of which
he or she is a director, officer, parfirer, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section
87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b)). The material you provide contains a copy of the lease agreement
between Mr. McGrath and Dr. Eiskamp concerning the Santa Cruz property. This agreement discloses the
existence of the Eiskamp Family Limited Parbrership. The facts do not discloseo however, whether this
limited parhership is presently in existence, and if so, if it is a business entity that is an economic interest
to Dr. Eiskamp under the foregoing standards. Dr. Eiskamp should consider this question in determining
his economic interests under the Act.
Interests in Real Property
The general rule is that an official's interest in real property is considered to be
directly involved in a decision if it meets any of the criteria in regulation 18704.2(a).
Regulation 1870a.2@) provides that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if "[t]he real property in
which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is located within 500
feet of the boundaries (or the_proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of
the governmental decision." T The real property that is thcsubjeci of the collection suit is
Mr. McGrath's property on which is located the groundwaterpumping facilities subject
to the augmentation charges for which collection is sought. This property adjoins Dr.
Eiskamp's santa Cnnpropefi. Since Dr. Eiskamp's Santa cruzproperfi is located
within 500 feet of the boundaries of the property which is the subject of governmental
decision, the Santa Cruz property is deemed tobe directly involved in PV![MA's
decisions concerning the collection suit.
Since Dr. Eiskamp's interest in his jointly-owned real property in Monterey
County does not meet any of the provisions of regulation 18704.2(a) his interest in this
real property is deemed tobe indirectly involved in such decisions. (Regulation
t87oa.2@)Q).)
OUESTION 2: GROUNDWATER AUGMENTATION CHARGE SUIT
Under the criteria above, Mr. McGrath (a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp) is
also directly involved in PWIMA's decisions concerning this suit. All Dr. Eiskamp's
other economic interests are indirectly involved.
Steps 5. & 6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of these governmental decisions upon Dr.
Eiskamp's economic interests wiII meet this materiality standard?
According to your facts, Dr. Eiskamp has two economic interests that are directly
involved in collection-related litigation decisions. The first is Mr. McGrath and the
second is Dr. Eiskamp's Santa Cruzparcel.
Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect at all, even one pemy, on an
individual such as Mr. McGrath, who is a source of income to apublic official, when that
individual is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed
material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).) With respect to real property, regulation
18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on directly
involved real property that is an economic interest to a public official is presumed to be
7 Amendments to regulation 187}4.2were adopted by the Commission at its January 17,2003,
public meeting. The above-quoted laaguage is taken from amended regulation 18704.2(a), as adopted by
the Commission.
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the governmental decision will have any financial effect at all on the real property.
Any proof relied upon by a public official in this regard must be reasonable and
objective.
Underregulation 18706, an effect upon economic interests is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. A financial
effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 19S.)
Since, under the facts provided, the outcome of this suit will likely determine Mr.
McGrath's liability to pay approximately $36,000 in past due charges, decisions
concerning the prosecution of this collection action will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Mr. McGrath. Consequently, Dr. Eiskamp has a conflict of
interest disqualifoing him from making,participating in making, or influencing the
PVWMA's decisions concerning the collections suit. Having reached this result, it is
unnecessary to discuss in our advice the reasonably foreseeable material financial effects
of such decisions on Dr. Eiskamp's other directly and indirectly involved economic
interests.
OUESTION 2: GROUNDWATER AUGMENTATION CHARGE SUIT
Mr. McGrath is directly involved as a co-plaintiffin this suit. As noted above, he
is a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp and thus, if decisions conceming the groundwater
augmentation charge suit will have any reasonably foreseeable financial effect at all, even
one penny, upon Mr. McGrath, it is deemed material. Since this suit, like the suit above,
bears upon Mr. McGrath's liability to pay approximately $36,000 in groundwater
augmentation charges, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions by PVWMA
concerning its defense of this suit will have amaterial financial effect upon Mr. McGrath.
Having reached this result, it is unnecessary to discuss in our advice the
reasonably foreseeable material financial ef,flects of such decisions on Dr. Eiskamp,s
other directly and indirectly involved economic interests.
7. & 8. "Public Generally" and'6legally Required participation, Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Section 87103, regulation 18707(a).) Under this
exception, if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is affected by the governmental
decision in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public official, then the
official may participate in the decision. Step eight is an exception (regulation 18708) that
applies when the official is legally required to participate in the decision. Typically, this
exception is invoked when a quorum cannot be constituted without the participation of
the disqualified official. There is nothing in the facts you supply to suggest that either of
these exceptions apply to Dr. Eiskamp's involvement in these decisions. We have
enclosed for your information copies of regulations 18707, 18707.1, and,18707.2, which
address the public generally exception, and regulation 18708, which addresses the legally
required participation exception.

2. Does Dr. Eiskamp have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from making,

:::
defense in a suit brought against it by James Scurich and others regarding the legality of
the PVWMA' s "augmentation char ge"?

CONCLUSION: l. & 2. Yes. Dr. Eiskamp has a conflict of interest disqualifying him from

Tutt Nossaman, Guthner, Knox & Elliott, LLP 50 California Street -34do Floor San Francisco, CA 94111-4707 Your Request for Advice Our FiIe No. A-02-341 Dear Ms. Tutt: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Dr. John Gerhardt Eiskamp, Director of Division D, Pajaro valley water Management Agency ("PVV[MA") regardingthe conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'Act").' This letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have abeady taken place. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission') does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice; this advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FppC Ops. 7l; Govt. Code section 83114.) QUESTTONS l. Does Dr. Eiskamp have a conflict of interest disqualifring him from making, participating in making, or influencing the decisions of the PWIMA concerning PWIMA's collection action against William J. McGrath? 2. Does Dr. Eiskamp have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from making, participating in making, or influencing the decisions of the PW\rMA concerning its I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations. ::: File No. A-02-341 Page No. 2 defense in a suit brought against it by James Scurich and others regarding the legality of the PVWMA' s "augmentation char ge"? CONCLUSIONS l. & 2. Yes. Dr. Eiskamp has a conflict of interest disqualifying him from involvement in the decisions ofthe PWITMA concerning these suits. Such decisions will have areasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Mr. McGrath who, through W.J. McGrath Orchards, is a source of income to Dr. Eiskamp. FACTS PVWMA is apublic entity and a California Water Agency orgarized and operating under Chapter 124 of the Califomia Water Code. PVWMA was formed, among other reasons, to manage the ground and surface water resources within the pajaro Basin, situated within Monterey, santa cruz and, San Benito counties. According to the facts you supplied in a February 6,2003, telephone conversation with the Commission's stafi PV![MA's jurisdiction extends over approximately 70,000 acres, of which 30,000 acres are irrigated for agricultural use. You further stated that approximately 58,000 acre-feet of groundwater is used annually within PW1rMA's jurisdiction, which is extracted through approximately 800 privately-owned wells. This water supply is augmented by an additional2,000 acre-feet per year of surface water. Of the total, approximately 10,000 acre-feet of groundwater per year is used to supply municipal water, with the remainder supplying the agricultural needs of the jurisdiction. Dr. Eiskamp owns a parcel of real estate located in Santa Cruz County. Approximately 17 acres of this property is leased to W.J. McGrath Orchards pursuant to a 6-year lease signed by Mr. McGrath, which expires in2004. Payment under ihe
